specification.topic-statement: The REPL event loop multiplexes terminal input, LLM HTTP responses, control socket commands, and timed animations on a single thread using a unified select call with a dynamically calculated timeout.

scope.in-scope: The main loop structure, event source priority ordering, timeout calculation, frame rendering integration, signal handling integration, key injection draining, headless mode, and loop termination.
scope.boundary: What each frame render produces — layer composition, output buffer assembly, atomic TTY write — is covered by the layer rendering specification. This specification covers only when a render is triggered.

The buffer that stores programmatically injected bytes and the byte-draining protocol are covered by the key injection specification. This specification covers only how the loop integrates with that buffer.

What happens when a terminal byte is parsed, an LLM response arrives, or a control socket command is processed is covered by the input parser, provider, and control socket specifications respectively.

field.name: terminal context
field.note: holds the TTY file descriptor and terminal dimensions; tty_fd is -1 in headless mode

field.name: database context
field.note: primary database connection; null if database is not configured

field.name: command history
field.note: in-memory history structure shared across all agents

field.name: tool registry
field.note: registered tools available to all agents

field.name: fork pending
field.note: true while a fork operation is in progress; prevents concurrent forks

field.name: session id
field.note: database session identifier; 0 if no database

field.name: data
field.note: accumulated rendered content; grows on demand

field.name: capacity
field.note: allocated bytes; doubles when an append would exceed it

behavior.loop-structure:
The loop runs until one of the termination conditions is met. Each iteration executes the following phases in strict order:
1. Signal check: pending resize and quit signals are checked and handled before any blocking.
2. Key injection drain: if the key injection buffer contains pending bytes, one byte is drained, fed to the input parser, an action is dispatched, and a frame is rendered if the action was recognized. The iteration then restarts immediately (skipping the select call) until the buffer is empty.
3. Timeout calculation: the minimum of all active per-source timeouts is computed. See timeout calculation.
4. I/O multiplexing: `select` blocks on the TTY file descriptor, the control socket file descriptors, and all in-flight LLM HTTP connection descriptors (registered by the provider) until one or more become ready or the timeout expires.
5. If `select` returns with an error code of `EINTR`, signals are re-checked and the loop restarts; any other error breaks the loop.
6. If `select` times out (ready count is zero), the scroll detector timeout handler is invoked.
7. If the TTY file descriptor is ready and is a valid descriptor (not −1), one byte is read and processed through the input parser.
8. Control socket events are dispatched to the control socket handler.
9. LLM HTTP events are processed for all agents regardless of the ready count from `select`.
10. If the current agent's spinner is visible, the monotonic clock is checked and the spinner frame is advanced if the interval has elapsed; if the frame advanced, a frame is rendered.
11. All agents are polled for completed tool execution threads.
12. All agents are checked for deferred fork prompts; any found are dispatched to the LLM.
behavior.timeout-calculation:
The timeout passed to `select` is the minimum of all active per-source timeouts. A source contributes its timeout only when it is active; inactive sources are excluded from the minimum.
- **Spinner animation**: 80 ms when the current agent's spinner is visible. - **Tool execution polling**: 50 ms when any agent is in the executing-tool state. - **LLM HTTP**: the minimum timeout requested by all active provider HTTP sessions (varies by provider and network conditions). - **Scroll detector**: a variable timeout managed by the scroll detector subsystem.
If no source is active, the default timeout is 1000 ms. The loop never blocks indefinitely when any time-sensitive source is active.
behavior.event-source-priority:
Event sources are handled in the following priority order within each iteration:
1. Signals (SIGWINCH, SIGINT, SIGTERM) — checked synchronously before blocking. 2. Key injection buffer — drained before `select` if bytes are pending; each byte triggers a full restart of the loop. 3. Terminal TTY input — processed after `select` returns. 4. Control socket — processed after terminal input. 5. LLM HTTP (curl_multi) — processed after control socket, for all agents. 6. Spinner animation timer — polled after LLM HTTP processing. 7. Tool completion polling — polled after spinner check. 8. Deferred fork prompts — polled last.
The ready count returned by `select` is ignored when processing LLM HTTP events. The provider's `perform` function is called for every agent on every iteration. The provider handles the case where no work is available.
behavior.frame-rendering-triggers:
A frame is rendered only in response to specific events, not on every iteration. The events that trigger a render are:
- A key injection byte produces a recognized input action. - A terminal input byte produces a recognized input action. - The spinner frame advances. - An LLM response chunk is received for the current agent. - A tool execution completes for any agent. - A control socket scroll command is received. - A scroll detector timeout fires. - A SIGWINCH resize is handled.
behavior.headless-mode:
In headless mode the application runs without a terminal. The terminal context is initialized with a fixed size of 100 columns by 50 rows and a TTY file descriptor of −1.
The differences from normal mode are:
- The TTY file descriptor is never added to the `select` read set, so terminal input is never processed from a physical terminal. - Write errors when rendering frames are silently ignored (writing to −1 fails by definition but is expected). - Standard output and standard error are not redirected to `/dev/null` (unlike normal mode, where they are redirected to suppress interference with the alternate terminal screen). - The control socket is the only interactive I/O channel.
The framebuffer is still assembled and stored on every render, making it available to the control socket `read_framebuffer` command.
Despite no physical terminal, every event that normally triggers a render still assembles a framebuffer. The write attempt fails silently. This ensures the framebuffer stored for control socket reads reflects the current state.
behavior.signal-handling-integration:
Signal handlers set global volatile flags and return immediately. The loop polls these flags at the start of each iteration and after `select` returns `EINTR`.
- SIGWINCH: the resize flag is cleared, terminal dimensions are re-queried via an ioctl, the stored dimensions are updated, and a frame is rendered. - SIGINT and SIGTERM: the quit flag is cleared, all cached provider instances are released, and the quit flag on the REPL context is set to trigger loop termination.
SIGWINCH sets a volatile flag. On the next loop iteration the flag is cleared, new dimensions are queried, and a re-render is triggered. This defers non-async-signal-safe operations out of the signal handler.
behavior.termination:
The loop exits when any of the following conditions becomes true:
- The REPL quit flag is set (by a quit signal or a control socket exit command). - A terminal read returns zero bytes (EOF — terminal closed) or a non-`EINTR` error. - A fatal error is returned by `select`.
On exit, the control socket is destroyed. A talloc destructor on the REPL context waits for any running tool execution thread to complete before releasing resources.
behavior.framebuffer-storage: After each render, the assembled framebuffer (the complete sequence of terminal escape codes and text content that was written to the TTY) is stored on the REPL context, replacing the previous framebuffer. The cursor row and column at the time of the render are also stored. The control socket `read_framebuffer` command returns this stored buffer.
